h1Title: Ludwig Angina: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Ludwig angina is a rapidly spreading bilateral cellulitis of the submandibular, sublingual, and submental spaces that originates almost exclusively from an odontogenic source — most often an infected lower second or third molar. Named by Wilhelm Friedrich von Ludwig in 1836, it is not a true abscess but a brawny, woody-hard cellulitis with gas-forming bacteria that can displace the tongue upward and backward, producing airway obstruction within hours. Mortality was near 50% before the antibiotic era; with modern intensive care, early surgical decompression, and airway management, case-fatality rates now range from 0-8%. Any patient with floor-of-mouth swelling, trismus, and a dental source should be treated as a potential Ludwig angina case until proven otherwise.

keyStats.prevalence: Exact incidence unknown; estimated 1-5 cases per 100,000 hospital admissions at tertiary centres; incidence rising with antibiotic-resistant odontogenic infections
keyStats.demographics: Adults aged 20-60 predominantly affected; male-to-female ratio approximately 3:1; patients with diabetes or HIV have higher rates of severe disease and longer hospital stay
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of presentation approximately 30-40 years in most published case series
keyStats.globalCases: Reported worldwide; higher incidence in resource-limited settings with poor dental access; over 200 cases per year documented at large referral centres in Nigeria and India

definition: Ludwig angina (ICD-10: K12.2 — cellulitis and abscess of mouth) is a bilateral rapidly progressive gangrenous cellulitis involving the submandibular, sublingual, and submental fascial spaces simultaneously. The bilateral involvement and synchronous spread across all three spaces distinguish it from single-space dental abscesses. The infection is characteristically polymicrobial — a mixed aerobic-anaerobic flora dominated by Streptococcus viridans group, Staphylococcus aureus, and anaerobes such as Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus, and Fusobacterium species. Gas-forming organisms (Clostridium, certain Bacteroides strains) produce crepitus detectable on palpation. The submandibular space communicates inferiorly with the carotid sheath, allowing infection to descend into the mediastinum if untreated — descending necrotising mediastinitis, a 40% mortality complication. The sublingual space contains the tongue, its musculature, and the hypoglossal and lingual nerves; swelling here elevates and displaces the tongue, causing the hallmark 'hot potato voice' and dysphagia before producing complete airway obstruction. Oral and maxillofacial surgeons, anaesthesiologists, and critical-care teams manage this condition jointly; airway management takes priority over all other interventions.

typesClassification.description: Bilateral submandibular, sublingual, and submental cellulitis with preserved but threatened airway; amenable to awake fibre-optic intubation or nasopharyngeal airway as a bridge, combined with aggressive IV antibiotics and surgical decompression.

A discrete fluid collection has formed within the cellulitis (present in 60-68% of cases on CT); requires incision and drainage in addition to antibiotics. Abscess formation may allow earlier resolution of cellulitis.

typesClassification.type: Complicated Ludwig angina with mediastinal spread
typesClassification.description: Infection has descended through the deep cervical fascia into the superior mediastinum — descending necrotising mediastinitis. Requires thoracic surgical consultation, mediastinal drainage, and carries 20-40% mortality even with treatment.

Patients with uncontrolled diabetes, HIV, haematological malignancy, or long-term steroid use develop more severe, rapidly-progressive disease, higher rates of fungal co-infection, and greater mortality.

primarySymptoms: Painful bilateral swelling of the floor of the mouth and submandibular region producing a characteristic 'bull-neck' appearance, often with woody-hard, non-fluctuant induration on palpation.
Elevation and posterior displacement of the tongue, causing progressive difficulty speaking, chewing, and swallowing — the hallmark 'hot potato voice'.
Trismus (restricted mouth opening, typically less than 2 cm inter-incisal distance) from spread of cellulitis to the masseter and pterygoid muscles.
Drooling of saliva with inability to swallow secretions due to extreme pharyngeal swelling.
High fever (above 38.5°C) and systemic toxaemia including rigors, tachycardia, hypotension, and in severe cases altered consciousness indicating septic shock.
Stridor, grunting, or laboured breathing with use of accessory muscles — late, life-threatening signs of impending airway obstruction.
Crepitus on palpating the floor of the mouth or submandibular skin — pathognomonic of gas-forming organisms, particularly Clostridium species.
Cervical swelling extending from the mandible toward the clavicle when infection spreads into the carotid sheath and parapharyngeal space.
Dental pain, decayed lower molar, or recent dental extraction in the preceding days to weeks in the majority of cases, pointing to the odontogenic source.
earlyWarningSigns: Worsening dental pain from a lower molar combined with new floor-of-mouth swelling and trismus — this combination should trigger emergency assessment, not watchful waiting.
Fever greater than 38°C and a stiff neck with submandibular firmness after a dental infection or extraction — early spreading odontogenic cellulitis.
A patient who reports their 'tongue feels big' or they cannot swallow saliva after a recent dental infection — displacement of the tongue indicates submandibular and sublingual space involvement.
emergencySigns: Stridor, respiratory distress, or oxygen saturation falling below 94% in a patient with floor-of-mouth swelling — imminent airway obstruction; call anaesthetics immediately for awake fibre-optic intubation or surgical airway.

Tachycardia above 120/min, hypotension (systolic BP below 90 mmHg), and fever — septic shock from odontogenic source requires emergent broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, and ICU admission.
Chest pain, pleuritic pain, or new dyspnoea in a patient with neck infection — descending necrotising mediastinitis must be excluded urgently with contrast-enhanced CT of the neck and chest.
Crepitus or crackling sensation over the submandibular skin — gas-forming organisms necessitate immediate aggressive surgical debridement under general anaesthesia.

causes.cause: Odontogenic infection from lower second or third molar
causes.description: Periapical abscess of the mandibular second or third molar is the precipitating cause in 70-90% of cases. The roots of these teeth lie below the attachment of the mylohyoid muscle, allowing infection to spread directly into the submandibular space without natural barriers. The lower first molar contributes approximately 10-15% of cases.

Lower molar extraction creates a direct portal of entry for oral flora into the submandibular space, particularly in immunocompromised patients. Delayed healing and dry socket can precede Ludwig angina in the weeks following an extraction.

Untreated pericoronitis around a partially erupted lower wisdom tooth allows polymicrobial flora access to the masticator and submandibular spaces, especially if the overlying tissue is repeatedly traumatised by the opposing upper tooth.

causes.cause: Mandibular fracture through infected third molar
causes.description: Traumatic mandibular fractures through a carious or infected tooth introduce oral bacteria directly into the submandibular space and bone; failure to provide antibiotic prophylaxis and early fixation substantially increases the risk.

Submandibular duct obstruction by a calculus (sialolith) causes ascending sialadenitis that can, if severe and untreated, progress to suppurative infection in the submandibular space mimicking or merging with Ludwig angina.

Diabetes mellitus, HIV infection, haematological malignancy, and long-term steroid use impair neutrophil function and mucosal integrity, allowing odontogenic flora to spread rapidly across fascial planes that would normally localise infection.

riskFactors.factor: Poor dental health and delayed dental care
riskFactors.description: Multiple carious or periodontally diseased lower molars are the substrate for Ludwig angina; populations with limited dental access — rural, low-income, uninsured — carry disproportionate risk.

Diabetic patients with HbA1c above 8% have impaired neutrophil chemotaxis and bacterial killing, contributing to a 2-3-fold higher rate of severe odontogenic infections and longer hospitalisation in published case series.
CD4 count below 200 cells/µL severely impairs immune response to odontogenic pathogens; Ludwig angina in HIV-positive patients has higher rates of unusual organisms and mediastinal spread.
Impaired mucosal and immune defences facilitate rapid bacterial spread; malnutrition is particularly prevalent among Ludwig angina patients in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia.
Men are affected approximately 3 times more frequently than women in most case series; attributed to higher rates of carious lower molars and delayed dental presentation.
Peak incidence corresponds to the age of wisdom-tooth problems and neglected caries; elderly patients are at risk from periodontal disease and post-extraction complications.
Long-term corticosteroid therapy and other immunosuppressants impair the host response; patients on these agents presenting with dental infection require a lower threshold for hospital admission.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 20-60 are the predominant group; the condition is uncommon in children but has been reported.
Male-to-female ratio approximately 3:1 in most Western and African case series.
Patients with diabetes account for 20-40% of admissions at referral centres in India and sub-Saharan Africa.
HIV-positive patients represent a significant minority in high-prevalence regions; atypical organisms and mediastinal spread are more frequent in this group.
diagnosisOverview: Ludwig angina is primarily a clinical diagnosis. The combination of bilateral submandibular induration, floor-of-mouth elevation, trismus, dysphagia, and an identifiable odontogenic source in a febrile patient is diagnostic without imaging. Clinical assessment must answer three questions immediately: Is the airway safe? Is there a discrete abscess amenable to drainage? Has infection spread to the neck or chest? Airway patency is evaluated by asking the patient to phonate, observing tongue position, and measuring inter-incisal distance; any deterioration requires anaesthetic assessment before imaging. Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck and chest from the skull base to the carina is the gold-standard imaging investigation when airway is preserved — it maps the fascial planes involved, identifies fluid collections amenable to drainage (60-68% of cases), detects gas in tissues, and identifies mediastinal extension. CT performed before airway management must not delay it. Blood investigations include full blood count, CRP, PCT, BMP, lactate, and blood cultures before antibiotics. Orthopantomogram or periapical radiograph identifies the causative tooth. Microbiological cultures should be taken from abscess fluid at the time of surgical drainage; swabs from the buccal mucosa are unreliable. Microbiology typically reveals 3-6 organisms — a mixed aerobic-anaerobic picture dominated by viridans streptococci, Bacteroides, and Fusobacterium.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical airway assessment and Mallampati scoring
diagnosticTests.purpose: Immediately assess whether the patient can protect their airway before proceeding with imaging or other investigations
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Clinician observes mouth opening, tongue elevation, and phonation. Takes under 2 minutes. Repeated frequently as the clinical picture can change rapidly.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines fascial space involvement, identifies drainable fluid collections, detects gas and mediastinal spread
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: IV contrast injection followed by a 10-15 second CT scan; takes under 30 minutes total from preparation to report. Performed only if the airway is safe.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, CRP, procalcitonin, and lactate
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies severity of infection and detects sepsis; white count above 15,000/µL and CRP above 100 mg/L confirm significant bacterial load
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venepuncture; results within 1-2 hours in an emergency. Lactate above 2 mmol/L indicates sepsis requiring intensive care.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bacteraemia and guides definitive antibiotic therapy; positive in approximately 20-30% of Ludwig angina admissions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two sets drawn from different venepuncture sites before the first antibiotic dose; results in 24-72 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Surgical wound swab or aspiration culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the specific polymicrobial flora in the fascial spaces and guides antibiotic de-escalation after sensitivity results
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sample taken at the time of surgical incision and drainage in the operating room; sent in anaerobic and aerobic transport media.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative carious or periapically infected mandibular tooth
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Brief dental X-ray scan; performed once the airway and haemodynamic status are stable, often immediately before or after CT.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of Ludwig angina requires simultaneous management of the airway, systemic infection, and the surgical source — these are not sequential steps. Airway management has absolute priority: awake fibre-optic nasotracheal intubation is the preferred technique when the airway is threatened but still patent, because it allows the patient to maintain muscle tone during intubation; rigid laryngoscopy is often impossible due to trismus and tongue displacement. In severe cases or failed fibre-optic intubation, emergency tracheostomy under local anaesthesia is performed. Once the airway is secured, IV antibiotics are started immediately. The current evidence-based regimen is high-dose benzylpenicillin (penicillin G) or co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin-clavulanate) plus metronidazole to cover the polymicrobial flora; clindamycin is substituted for penicillin-allergic patients. Anti-MRSA agents (vancomycin, linezolid) are added where community-acquired MRSA is prevalent locally or when blood cultures return positive for MRSA. Corticosteroids (dexamethasone 8 mg IV) reduce oedema in selected cases, facilitating extubation within 48-72 hours, though evidence is limited to observational studies. Surgical decompression — incision and drainage — is performed under general anaesthesia with the airway already secured. External cervical incisions drain the submandibular and submental spaces bilaterally; intra-oral incisions through the floor of the mouth drain the sublingual space. Drains are left in place for 24-48 hours. The causative tooth is extracted at the same surgical episode where possible. Mediastinal spread requires additional thoracic surgical drainage, either via cervicotomy extended below the clavicle, video-assisted thoracoscopy, or open thoracotomy depending on extent. ICU care post-operatively monitors for septic shock, multi-organ dysfunction, and reintubation requirements. Mean hospital stay is 5-10 days in uncomplicated cases and 14-21 days with mediastinal involvement.

medicalTreatments.name: IV benzylpenicillin (2.4 g every 4 hours) plus metronidazole (500 mg IV every 8 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line regimen covering oral streptococci and obligate anaerobes. Given immediately on diagnosis, before culture results, targeting the polymicrobial flora.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical improvement (reduced fever, CRP decline) within 48-72 hours of source control in 80-90% of cases in published series

medicalTreatments.name: IV co-amoxiclav (1.2 g every 8 hours) as single-agent alternative
medicalTreatments.description: Amoxicillin-clavulanate covers viridans streptococci, anaerobes including Bacteroides species, and many Gram-negative organisms. Simpler dosing schedule than penicillin-metronidazole combination.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable clinical cure rates to penicillin-metronidazole combination in observational cohort studies

medicalTreatments.name: IV clindamycin (600 mg every 6 hours) for penicillin allergy
medicalTreatments.description: Provides anaerobic and streptococcal coverage in penicillin or amoxicillin-allergic patients. Monitor for Clostridioides difficile colitis with prolonged courses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response rates comparable to beta-lactam regimens in small retrospective series; risk of C. difficile colitis approximately 2-4 times higher than beta-lactams

medicalTreatments.name: Dexamethasone (8 mg IV once or twice daily for 2-3 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive agent to reduce inflammatory oedema in the sublingual and submandibular spaces, potentially facilitating earlier extubation and reduced ICU stay. Used with antibiotics, not as sole therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational series report 24-48 hour earlier extubation vs no steroid; no randomised trial data available

surgicalOptions.name: Bilateral external cervical incision and drainage
surgicalOptions.description: Horizontal skin incisions placed at the level of the hyoid bilaterally in the submandibular space; blunt dissection opens the fascial planes and any pockets of pus are evacuated. Drains left in place for 24-48 hours.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution of cellulitis in over 90% of cases when performed with appropriate antibiotics; re-exploration rate approximately 10-15% for persistent collections

surgicalOptions.name: Intra-oral incision and drainage of sublingual space
surgicalOptions.description: Longitudinal incision through the floor of the mouth mucosa medial to Wharton's duct provides access to the sublingual space. Combined with external drainage for complete decompression.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Used as part of comprehensive drainage in nearly all Ludwig angina surgical series; standalone intra-oral drainage inadequate for bilateral spread

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency tracheostomy under local anaesthesia
surgicalOptions.description: Performed when the airway cannot be managed by fibre-optic intubation due to extreme swelling or failed intubation. Provides a definitive, secure airway for the duration of ICU stay and allows unrestricted surgical access to the neck.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Life-saving procedure; overall mortality in Ludwig angina with early tracheostomy and surgical decompression is 0-8% at specialised centres

surgicalOptions.name: Extraction of causative tooth
surgicalOptions.description: Performed simultaneously with incision and drainage under general anaesthesia. Removes the ongoing source of infection.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Source removal is essential to prevent infection recurrence; performed in the same operation in most cases if tooth access permits

alternativeTreatments.name: Hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) therapy as adjunct for necrotising infection
alternativeTreatments.description: Reported in isolated case reports as adjunct therapy where gas-forming organisms or early necrotising fasciitis features are identified; enhances neutrophil killing of anaerobes. Evidence limited to case reports; not standard of care but considered at centres with HBO availability.

whySeeSpecialist: Ludwig angina requires immediate joint management by oral and maxillofacial surgery, anaesthesiology, and intensive care. General practitioners and general dentists must not attempt drainage in outpatient settings — an unsecured airway can become completely obstructed within minutes of manipulation. All suspected cases should be sent directly to the emergency department by ambulance.
doctorSelectionGuide: Care requires a hospital with oral and maxillofacial surgery on call 24/7, an anaesthesiologist experienced in difficult airway management, and an ICU. Tertiary referral centres with head-and-neck surgery units manage the highest volumes and have the best outcomes for complicated cases including mediastinal spread.
hospitalCriteria: 24-hour oral and maxillofacial or head-and-neck surgical cover
Anaesthesiologist experienced with awake fibre-optic intubation and difficult airway
CT scanner accessible at any time for emergency neck and chest imaging
Intensive care unit capability for post-operative monitoring
Thoracic surgical consultation available for mediastinal complications
keyFacilities: Tertiary hospital emergency departments with head-and-neck surgical capacity
University hospital oral and maxillofacial departments with 24-hour cover
preventionStrategies: Treat carious lower molars early, before periapical infection develops — the single most effective preventive measure.
Ensure adequate antibiotic prophylaxis for lower-molar extractions in patients with diabetes, HIV, or other immunosuppressive conditions.
Seek dental evaluation at the first sign of lower-jaw dental pain, especially if accompanied by floor-of-mouth swelling.
Maintain glycaemic control in patients with diabetes to reduce both dental infection susceptibility and severity of spread.
Primary dental care access — Ludwig angina is largely a disease of dental neglect; systemic public health investment in dental services prevents severe cases.
lifestyleModifications: After discharge, complete the full oral antibiotic course prescribed on leaving hospital.
Attend dental follow-up within 2-4 weeks of discharge to address all remaining carious or periodontally compromised lower molar teeth.
Optimise diabetes control with the endocrinology team if HbA1c was elevated at admission.
Avoid smoking — tobacco impairs mucosal and wound healing and is associated with more severe deep-space dental infections.
Return to the emergency department immediately if floor-of-mouth swelling, fever, or difficulty swallowing recurs after discharge.

recommended: High-protein liquid or semi-liquid diet during and immediately after hospitalisation to support immune function and tissue healing
Adequate micronutrients — zinc (8-11 mg/day), vitamin C (75-90 mg/day) — to support wound healing
Adequate hydration (at least 2 L per day) once oral intake is re-established post-operatively
Iron-rich foods if anaemia was identified at admission
avoid: Alcohol during antibiotic treatment and for one week post-discharge
Tobacco in all forms throughout recovery
Tough, chewy, or hard foods until jaw pain and trismus have fully resolved
Sugar-dense foods and drinks that promote further dental caries in the recovery period

exerciseGuidelines: Complete rest during the acute phase of hospitalisation. Light activity (walking around the ward) is encouraged from day 3-5 as fever and airway risk decline. Return to normal activity within 2-4 weeks of discharge for uncomplicated cases; longer rehabilitation for mediastinal complications.
dailyManagement: Complete the full course of oral antibiotics after discharge — typically 7-10 days of amoxicillin-clavulanate or co-amoxiclav.
Perform gentle warm saline mouth rinses three to four times daily to keep the oral and surgical wounds clean.
Monitor the neck and floor of the mouth daily for any returning swelling, hardness, or pain — immediate re-presentation if these signs appear.
Attend wound review at 5-7 days post-discharge; surgical drains should have been removed in hospital.
Keep diabetes or immunosuppressive conditions optimally managed as a long-term priority to prevent recurrence.
prognosis: Prognosis in Ludwig angina depends heavily on speed of recognition, airway management, and source control. Contemporary series from high-resource settings report mortality of 0-8% with early surgical decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and ICU care — a dramatic reduction from the near-50% mortality of the pre-antibiotic era. Mediastinal spread (descending necrotising mediastinitis) increases mortality to 20-40% even in well-resourced centres, because by the time infection reaches the mediastinum, extensive tissue necrosis and multi-organ failure may already be established. Predictors of poor outcome include delayed presentation beyond 48 hours, gas on CT imaging, diabetes with poor glycaemic control, bilateral involvement at admission, and descending infection. In survivors, long-term sequelae are uncommon: most patients recover full mouth opening, swallowing function, and neck mobility within 4-8 weeks. Permanent tracheostomy is rarely required in uncomplicated cases; the tracheostomy stoma closes within days of decannulation. Recurrence after source-tooth extraction and appropriate antibiotic therapy is unusual.
recoveryTimeline: Fever and systemic toxaemia typically improve within 48-72 hours of source control and IV antibiotics. Trismus resolves over 1-4 weeks as swelling subsides. Hospital stay averages 5-10 days for uncomplicated cases, 14-21 days with mediastinal involvement. Physiotherapy for jaw and neck mobility may be needed after prolonged intubation.
complications: Descending necrotising mediastinitis — the most feared complication; occurs in approximately 3-10% of Ludwig angina cases; mortality 20-40%; requires thoracic surgical drainage.
Airway obstruction and asphyxia — the immediate life-threatening complication; prevented only by early anaesthetic assessment and timely intubation or tracheostomy.
Septic shock and multi-organ failure — develops in approximately 10-20% of hospitalised cases; managed with fluid resuscitation, vasopressors, and ICU care.
Aspiration pneumonia — may develop after tongue displacement impairs swallowing, particularly before a definitive airway is established.
Cavernous sinus thrombosis via haematogenous spread — rare; presents as headache, proptosis, and visual changes in a patient with Ludwig angina; requires anticoagulation and intracranial imaging.

Persistent trismus and reduced mouth opening — occurs in a small proportion of patients from scar tissue after extensive surgical decompression; treated with physiotherapy and mouth-opening exercises.

supportResources.name: American Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons (AAOMS)
supportResources.description: Patient information on deep-space dental infections, jaw surgery, and emergency oral surgery from the North American specialty body.

supportResources.name: NHS — Dental Abscess (and spreading infection)
supportResources.description: UK NHS patient guidance on dental abscess recognition and when to seek emergency care for spreading infection.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peritonsillar Abscess (Quinsy)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peritonsillar abscess causes unilateral swelling in the soft palate, uvular deviation, trismus, and a 'hot potato voice', similar to Ludwig angina. However, peritonsillar abscess is unilateral and originates from tonsillar tissue, not a dental source; it does not produce bilateral floor-of-mouth induration or the woody-hard submandibular swelling characteristic of Ludwig angina. Drainage of a peritonsillar abscess is performed intra-orally at the peritonsillar space; Ludwig angina requires bilateral external cervical incisions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epiglottitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both can present with drooling, dysphagia, and airway compromise. Epiglottitis shows an enlarged, cherry-red epiglottis on laryngoscopy and the 'thumb sign' on lateral neck X-ray; it does not produce submandibular induration or trismus. Ludwig angina has a clear submandibular component and usually a dental precipitant.

confusedWithConditions.name: Localized Odontogenic Abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A localized dental abscess involves a single tooth and its immediate surrounding tissue, is usually fluctuant, and can often be drained and treated in a dental chair under local anaesthesia. Ludwig angina is bilateral, involves all three submandibular spaces, is characteristically non-fluctuant and woody-hard, and always requires hospital admission and airway planning.

relatedConditions.name: Odontogenic Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Ludwig angina is the most severe expression of a spreading odontogenic infection originating from the lower molars.

relatedConditions.name: Impacted Wisdom Teeth
relatedConditions.relevance: Infected impacted lower wisdom teeth are a common precipitant of Ludwig angina through spread of pericoronitis to adjacent fascial spaces.

relatedConditions.name: Peritonsillar Abscess
relatedConditions.relevance: Another deep-space head-and-neck infection that can cause airway compromise; key differential and occasional co-presenting condition in parapharyngeal space infections.

faqs.question: What is Ludwig angina?
faqs.answer: Ludwig angina is a life-threatening bilateral bacterial cellulitis of the submandibular, sublingual, and submental spaces, almost always originating from an infected lower molar tooth. It causes woody-hard floor-of-mouth swelling, tongue displacement, trismus, and can block the airway within hours if untreated.

faqs.question: Is Ludwig angina a dental infection?
faqs.answer: Yes, in 70-90% of cases. Ludwig angina starts as a periapical abscess from a carious or infected lower second or third molar and spreads rapidly across the fascial spaces of the floor of the mouth and neck. Early treatment of lower molar infections prevents it.

faqs.question: Why is Ludwig angina dangerous?
faqs.answer: The infection causes rapid swelling that pushes the tongue upward and backward, obstructing the airway. Before antibiotics and modern intensive care, it was fatal in up to 50% of cases. Even today, mortality is 0-8% without mediastinal spread and 20-40% if infection reaches the chest.

faqs.question: What are the signs of Ludwig angina?
faqs.answer: Key signs are bilateral swelling and firmness of the floor of the mouth and neck, elevated tongue, hot-potato voice, drooling, trismus, high fever, and severe dental pain. Stridor or difficulty breathing is an emergency sign indicating airway obstruction.

faqs.question: How is Ludwig angina treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment requires simultaneous airway management, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (penicillin or co-amoxiclav plus metronidazole), and surgical incision and drainage in the operating room. The causative tooth is extracted at the same operation. ICU monitoring follows.

faqs.question: Which antibiotics are used for Ludwig angina?
faqs.answer: IV benzylpenicillin plus metronidazole or IV co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin-clavulanate) are first-line regimens, covering the polymicrobial oral flora. Clindamycin replaces penicillin in allergic patients. Anti-MRSA cover (vancomycin) is added where MRSA is prevalent.

faqs.question: Can Ludwig angina be treated with antibiotics alone?
faqs.answer: Antibiotics alone are insufficient in most cases. Surgical decompression is required to evacuate the infected spaces and remove the causative tooth. Antibiotics without drainage carry a high risk of airway compromise and mediastinal spread.

faqs.question: How do you diagnose Ludwig angina?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is primarily clinical: bilateral submandibular induration, tongue elevation, trismus, and a dental source in a febrile patient. Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck and chest confirms fascial plane involvement, identifies fluid collections, and detects mediastinal spread.

faqs.question: What is the mortality rate of Ludwig angina?
faqs.answer: With modern treatment at experienced centres, mortality is 0-8% for uncomplicated cases. If infection spreads to the mediastinum (descending necrotising mediastinitis), mortality rises to 20-40% despite aggressive management.

faqs.question: Who is at highest risk for Ludwig angina?
faqs.answer: Patients with carious lower molars who delay dental care are at highest risk. Diabetes, HIV, and other immunosuppressive conditions dramatically increase the severity of spread. Men aged 20-60 account for most cases.

faqs.question: Does Ludwig angina require ICU admission?
faqs.answer: Yes in most cases. Airway monitoring, IV antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, and post-operative observation after surgical drainage require intensive care facilities. The airway can deteriorate rapidly even after initial improvement.

faqs.question: How can Ludwig angina be prevented?
faqs.answer: The primary prevention is treating lower molar dental disease early, before a periapical abscess develops. Patients with diabetes or immune compromise should receive prophylactic antibiotics for lower molar extractions and seek dental assessment urgently for any jaw pain.

faqs.question: What is descending necrotising mediastinitis?
faqs.answer: Descending necrotising mediastinitis is a complication of Ludwig angina where infection tracks down the deep cervical fascia into the chest. It causes chest pain, respiratory failure, and septic shock, and requires combined neck and thoracic surgical drainage with mortality of 20-40%.

faqs.question: What does crepitus mean in Ludwig angina?
faqs.answer: Crepitus — a crackling or bubbling sensation felt on pressing the neck skin — indicates gas-forming bacteria such as Clostridium species within the tissues. Its presence confirms a severe infection requiring immediate surgical debridement in the operating room.

faqs.question: How long is the hospital stay for Ludwig angina?
faqs.answer: Uncomplicated cases average 5-10 days. Patients with mediastinal spread or requiring tracheostomy may stay 14-21 days or longer. Discharge follows documented clinical improvement, negative blood cultures, and established oral antibiotic therapy.

regionalNames.name: Phlegmon du plancher de la bouche

metaDescription: Ludwig angina: a fast-spreading deep neck infection that can block the airway. Symptoms, urgent diagnosis and surgical treatment reviewed here.

sources.title: Boscolo-Rizzo P, Da Mosto MC. 2009. Submandibular space infection: a potentially lethal infection. Int J Infect Dis 13(3):327-33.

Lemonovich TL, Schmitt SK. 2012. Serious dental infection and clinical presentation of deep space neck infections. Oral Maxillofac Surg Clin North Am 24(2):211-27.
Bridwell RE, Koyfman A, Long B. 2021. High risk and low prevalence diseases: Ludwig angina. Am J Emerg Med 49:77-83.
Bahl R, Sandhu S, Singh K, Sahai N, Mehta M. 2014. Odontogenic infections: microbiology and management. Contemp Clin Dent 5(3):307-11.
